meta.question: How should we scale Remote Farmer for multi-session and multi-sprint support, and what repo cartography system keeps AI model interactions fast as the Wheat codebase and git history grow?

claims.content: Must support multiple concurrent Claude Code sessions in a single Farmer dashboard — each session identifiable, filterable, and independently approvable.
Dashboard must support viewing and switching between multiple Wheat sprints — each sprint's claims, compilation status, and output artifacts accessible from the remote UI.

Repo must be self-describing — new Claude Code sessions should understand the repo structure without full-tree Glob/Grep scans. A manifest or index system must point AI models to the right files quickly.
Zero npm dependencies. Build on existing Remote Farmer prototype. Must not break single-session workflow.
AI model search and context usage must be measurably faster with cartography than without. Sprint structure must scale to 10+ sprints and 500+ claims without degrading model response time.

Every Claude Code hook payload includes session_id (UUID format), transcript_path, cwd, and hook_event_name as common fields. The session_id is a stable UUID assigned at session creation and persists across resume/continue. This is the natural routing key for multi-session Farmer — no custom identification needed.

Claude Code supports SessionStart and SessionEnd lifecycle hooks. SessionStart fires on new sessions and resumes (with source field: 'startup', 'resume', 'clear', 'compact'). SessionEnd fires on session termination but cannot block it. These provide explicit session lifecycle signals — no need to infer session boundaries from tool-use gaps.

Use single-server, session-keyed state architecture: one Map<session_id, SessionState> where SessionState holds pending permissions, activity log, trust level, and session rules independently. This is the simplest path that satisfies d001 without breaking d004 (zero deps, backwards compat). Multiple server instances add port management complexity with no benefit for a localhost dashboard.

The current server stores global singletons (pending, activity, messages, trustLevel, sessionRules) that must become per-session. Minimal refactor: create a sessions Map, and a getSession(id) function that lazily initializes SessionState on first hook from a new session_id. The SSE broadcast should tag every message with session_id so the dashboard can filter/route client-side.

Dashboard UX should use a session-aware unified feed as default view, with session color-coding (assign each session_id a stable hue via hash). Add a session switcher pill bar at top for filtering to one session. Avoid split-view on mobile — phone screen real estate is too limited. Permission cards already show truncated session_id (first 8 chars); extend this with color dots and cwd-derived labels (e.g., 'wheat', 'api-server').

Concurrent permission requests from multiple sessions create phone-UX pressure: if two sessions both need approval within seconds, the user must context-switch between unrelated tool calls. Risk of approving the wrong session's action. Mitigation: permission cards must prominently display session label + cwd, and use distinct session colors. Consider audio/haptic differentiation per session.

Session lifecycle detection: SessionStart hook fires with source='startup' for new sessions and source='resume' for continued ones. SessionEnd hook fires on termination but cannot block it. For sessions that crash without firing SessionEnd, implement a heartbeat timeout (e.g., 5 minutes with no hook activity = session marked stale). The server already has a 120s timeout for individual permissions; session-level staleness needs a longer window.

Refactoring server.mjs from global state to session-keyed Maps is a ~100-line change (add SessionState class, getSession helper, update 6 handler functions to pass session context). Dashboard HTML changes are larger (~200 lines) for session switcher UI and color-coding. Total estimated effort: 2-4 hours for a working multi-session prototype, assuming SessionStart/SessionEnd hooks are configured to POST to new /hooks/lifecycle endpoint.

Claude Code automatically loads CLAUDE.md from the project root and from any subdirectory the user is working in. This makes CLAUDE.md files the highest-bandwidth zero-cost channel for repo cartography — anything in CLAUDE.md is in context before the first tool call. Per-directory CLAUDE.md files enable layered discovery without a central mega-file.

Maintain a single machine-readable manifest file (wheat-manifest.json) at the repo root that maps topics to file paths, claim ID ranges to sprints, and prototype names to directories. Claude Code can Read this one file (~1-2KB) to know where everything is, replacing multi-step Glob+Grep discovery. The manifest should be auto-regenerated by the wheat compiler on every compilation so it never drifts.

Claude Code's Read tool costs one tool call regardless of file size (up to 2000 lines). Glob and Grep each cost one call but may require multiple rounds. A pre-computed index consumed in a single Read eliminates the iterative Glob->Read->Grep->Read pattern that costs 3-5 tool calls per lookup. At 10+ sprints (~300-500 files) the difference becomes significant.

Add a sprint-index section to compilation.json (or separate sprints-index.json) listing all sprints — current and archived in examples/ — with question, phase, claim count, date range, and file paths. Lets Claude Code answer 'which sprint dealt with X?' with a single Read instead of scanning examples/*/claims.json.

Extend commit message conventions with topic tags in brackets: 'wheat: /research [cartography] summary — r010-r012'. Makes git log --grep='[cartography]' instant. Combined with existing 'git log --oneline claims.json' pattern, gives two fast search axes: by topic and by file.

Per-directory CLAUDE.md files create maintenance burden: they must stay synchronized with actual directory contents. If a CLAUDE.md claims 'this directory contains X' but X was moved, it actively misleads the AI — worse than no index. Mitigation: generate per-directory CLAUDE.md content from the manifest automatically, or use manifest as single source of truth and keep CLAUDE.md for behavioral instructions only.

At current scale (80 files, 5 claims, 1 sprint), cartography adds overhead without measurable benefit — Glob/Grep return results in <1s. Crossover point where a manifest saves net time: ~150-200 files or 3+ sprints, when Glob results become noisy enough to require follow-up filtering.

Structure the manifest as a topic map rather than a file tree. AI agents search by concept ('where is the SSE prototype?', 'what claims about security?'), not by directory path. Map topic -> {claims: [id range], files: [paths], sprint: name} so a Read of the manifest directly answers conceptual queries.

r016 states '80 files' but the repo actually contains 157 files (excluding .git). The repo has already crossed into r016's own predicted crossover zone of 150-200 files. The baseline measurement is wrong, which invalidates the 'overhead without measurable benefit' conclusion — the benefit zone is NOW, not future.

r016 frames the crossover as file-count-driven, but the real cost driver is result noise from structural duplication. At 157 files, 'dashboard.html' matches 10 files across examples/*/output, prototypes/*, and templates/. Grepping 'claims.json' returns 71 file matches. Grepping 'createServer' returns 5 hits across 3 near-identical copies. The examples/ directory alone holds 62 files that mirror active sprint files. This duplication means the noise problem is already acute — a manifest that says 'the current sprint's server is prototypes/remote-dashboard/server.mjs, ignore examples/' would eliminate 40-60% of false hits TODAY, not at some future scale.

source.artifact: Grep 'dashboard.html' => 40 files; Grep 'claims.json' => 71 files; examples/ = 62 files

claims.content: r016 measures cost as wall-clock Glob/Grep time (<1s) but ignores the dominant cost: context window pollution. When Grep returns 71 results for 'claims.json', the AI must process all paths in its context window to determine which is relevant. Each noisy tool result consumes ~500-2000 tokens of context. Over a multi-step research session with 20+ tool calls, noisy results can waste 10-40K tokens — equivalent to 5-20% of the context window. The crossover point for cartography is not about I/O latency, it is about context budget efficiency, and that threshold is much lower than r016 estimates because even one noisy result triggers follow-up tool calls that compound the cost.

Official Claude Code docs (code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks) confirm SessionStart and SessionEnd are supported hook event types. SessionStart input includes a `source` field with values 'startup', 'resume', 'clear', 'compact' — exactly as r002 states. SessionEnd cannot block termination (no decision control). SessionStart stdout is injected as context for Claude; only `type: "command"` hooks are supported for SessionStart. SessionEnd receives a `reason` field (not `source`) with values: 'clear', 'logout', 'prompt_input_exit', 'bypass_permissions_disabled', 'other'.

Claude Code supports at least 17 hook event types beyond SessionStart/SessionEnd: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, Notification, UserPromptSubmit, Stop, SubagentStart, SubagentStop, PreCompact, PermissionRequest, Setup, TeammateIdle, TaskCompleted, ConfigChange, WorktreeCreate, WorktreeRemove, InstructionsLoaded. Four hook handler types exist: command, http, prompt, and agent — but SessionStart and SessionEnd only support 'command' type. This is a design constraint for multi-session Farmer: SessionStart/SessionEnd hooks cannot use HTTP POST directly; they require a shell command wrapper (e.g., curl) to notify the Farmer server.
